does: This paper conducts a systematic comparative analysis of six bias mitigation techniques for controlling gender stereotypes in LLM outputs: prompt-only baselines, generate-and-filter, DFA-based Ctrl-G decoding, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Iterative Nullspace Projection (INLP). The authors evaluate these methods on a compositional constraint task requiring generated sentences to contain both agentic and communal descriptors for each of twenty Winogender-derived occupations. They measure performance across constraint compliance, lexical diversity, and fluency, revealing trade-offs between control strength and output naturalness. The core finding is that supervised fine-tuning approaches substantially outperform preference learning methods like DPO in achieving bias control while maintaining quality.
connects: This work builds on a long line of research documenting gender bias in LLMs, including foundational analyses like Rudinger et al. (2018) showing stereotyped language generation in occupation contexts. It extends prior work proposing diverse mitigation strategies including prompting techniques, constrained decoding methods (Dathathri et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2024), post-processing, and alignment approaches like DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) and nullspace projection methods (Ravfogel et al., 2022). Rather than proposing yet another new technique, this paper synthesizes understanding of existing approaches by directly comparing their efficacy, learning dynamics, and trade-offs under controlled conditions, filling a gap in comparative evaluation that prior scattered work had not addressed.
enables: This systematic comparison enables practitioners to make informed choices about bias mitigation strategies given specific constraints on control strength, naturalness, and computational resources. By demonstrating that supervised fine-tuning succeeds where preference learning struggles, the work suggests that alignment methods popular for safety may not directly transfer to fine-grained compositional constraint problems. Future work can build on these findings to develop hybrid approaches combining the strengths of different techniques, investigate why preference learning underperforms on compositional tasks, and extend the evaluation methodology to other bias types beyond gender stereotyping in occupational contexts.
